subject: EU Law
lesson_title: Article 263 TFEU: Standing to Challenge EU Acts

questions.question: Article 263 TFEU distinguishes between 'privileged', 'semi-privileged', and 'non-privileged' applicants. Which categories correspond to these three?

options.A: Privileged: EU institutions; Semi-privileged: member states; Non-privileged: private individuals
options.B: Privileged: member states, Council, Commission, Parliament (automatic standing); Semi-privileged: Court of Auditors, ECB, Committee of the Regions (standing to protect prerogatives); Non-privileged: natural and legal persons (standing only if directly and individually concerned)
options.C: Privileged: member states only; Semi-privileged: EU institutions; Non-privileged: third states
options.D: Privileged: all EU bodies; Semi-privileged: member states; Non-privileged: NGOs and civil society

questions.explanation: Article 263 creates three tiers: (1) Privileged applicants — member states, European Parliament, Council, and Commission have automatic standing (no need to show particular interest); (2) Semi-privileged applicants — Court of Auditors, ECB, and Committee of the Regions have standing only to protect their prerogatives (institutional interests); (3) Non-privileged applicants — natural and legal persons must show direct and individual concern (and for regulatory acts, direct concern without implementing measures).

questions.question: The test for 'individual concern' of a non-privileged applicant challenging a Regulation or a Decision addressed to another person was established in:

options.D: Union de Pequeños Agricultores (UPA) v Council [2002]

questions.explanation: Plaumann (Case 25/62) established the locus classicus test for individual concern: a private applicant is individually concerned only if the EU measure affects them by reason of attributes peculiar to them or circumstances in which they are differentiated from all other persons, and by virtue of these factors distinguishes them individually in the same way as an addressee. Clementine importers (like Plaumann) who were affected as any other trader in that category were not individually concerned — the test is extraordinarily restrictive.

questions.question: The Plaumann test for individual concern has been widely criticised as being too restrictive. The core criticism is:

options.A: It is too easy to satisfy — almost any affected party can show individual concern
options.B: It is almost impossible to satisfy for challenges to legislative acts of general application — the more general the act, the less likely it affects anyone 'individually' in Plaumann's sense, effectively precluding private party review of general measures
options.C: It does not apply to Regulations, only to Decisions
options.D: It was designed for a six-member Community and is outdated in a 27-member EU

questions.explanation: The Plaumann test is paradoxical: the more people a legislative act affects, the less any individual is 'individually' concerned — because they are not differentiated from the general class. This makes it almost impossible for private parties to challenge EU legislative acts of general application, even when they are directly harmed. Critics (including AG Jacobs in UPA) argued this leaves private parties without an adequate judicial remedy, potentially violating the right to effective judicial protection. The CJEU has maintained Plaumann despite repeated calls for reform.

questions.question: In Union de Pequeños Agricultores (UPA) v Council [2002], AG Jacobs proposed a reformulation of the Plaumann test. What did he suggest, and what did the Court decide?

options.A: AG Jacobs proposed making individual concern easier to establish; the Court adopted his approach
options.B: AG Jacobs proposed that 'individual concern' should be satisfied whenever an applicant is adversely affected by an EU measure; the Court rejected this and maintained Plaumann, directing concerns about access to justice to the legislature or to the national court/preliminary reference route
options.C: AG Jacobs proposed abolishing the individual concern requirement; the Court agreed in principle but held it required treaty amendment
options.D: AG Jacobs proposed that individual concern should be assessed by national courts, not the CJEU

questions.explanation: In UPA, AG Jacobs proposed a significant relaxation: individual concern should be established if the applicant's legal position is substantially affected by the measure. The CJEU declined, reaffirming Plaumann and holding that the solution to inadequate access to justice lay in: (1) the national court/preliminary reference route (national courts can refer questions of EU act validity to the CJEU); (2) member states strengthening national procedural rules for access to courts. This response was criticised as insufficient — the preliminary reference route is indirect and cumbersome.

questions.question: The Lisbon Treaty amended Article 263(4) TFEU to add a third limb for non-privileged applicants. What is this new limb?

options.A: Non-privileged applicants may challenge any act of the Commission where it affects their economic interests
options.B: Non-privileged applicants may institute proceedings against regulatory acts which are of direct concern to them and do not entail implementing measures — without needing to show individual concern
options.C: Non-privileged applicants may challenge legislative acts adopted without Parliament's consent
options.D: Non-privileged applicants may bring annulment actions if they obtain leave from a national court

questions.explanation: Article 263(4) TFEU (post-Lisbon) added a third limb: any natural or legal person may challenge 'a regulatory act which is of direct concern to them and does not entail implementing measures'. The critical innovation is the absence of the 'individual concern' requirement — only 'direct concern' is needed. This was intended to improve access to justice following UPA. However, the Court in Inuit Tapiriit Kanatami [2013] interpreted 'regulatory act' narrowly — it means non-legislative acts of general application (delegated/implementing acts), NOT legislative acts (Regulations, Directives adopted by OLP).

questions.question: The concept of 'direct concern' for standing purposes requires that:

options.A: The act directly names the applicant as its addressee
options.B: The EU measure directly affects the applicant's legal situation without the interposition of discretionary implementing measures — the challenged act must itself determine the applicant's legal position without leaving the member state or implementing authority any real discretion
options.C: The applicant has a direct contractual relationship with the EU institution that adopted the act
options.D: The applicant is located in the member state to which the act is addressed

questions.explanation: Direct concern requires two elements: (1) the measure must directly affect the applicant's legal position; (2) there must be no intervening discretionary implementation — the challenged act must leave no real discretion to the implementing authority (whether national or EU). If a member state has significant discretion in implementing the act, the applicant's position is not directly affected by the EU act itself but by the national implementing measure, and the proper challenge is in national courts (Alcan Deutschland).

questions.question: Inuit Tapiriit Kanatami v Parliament and Council [2013] is significant because it held that 'regulatory acts' in Article 263(4) TFEU means:

options.A: All acts of general application — including legislative acts adopted under the ordinary legislative procedure
options.B: Only non-legislative acts of general application — the Regulation banning seal product trade (adopted by OLP) was a legislative act, not a 'regulatory act', so the Inuit Tapiriit Kanatami required individual concern
options.C: Any act adopted by an EU regulatory agency
options.D: Acts of general application whose content regulates the internal market

questions.explanation: In Inuit Tapiriit Kanatami (C-583/11 P, 2013), the applicants challenged an EU Regulation banning seal product trade, arguing they were directly concerned by the regulatory act without need to show individual concern. The CJEU held that 'regulatory act' in Article 263(4) means non-legislative acts of general application (delegated acts under Article 290 and implementing acts under Article 291). The seal Regulation, adopted under OLP (a legislative act), was not a 'regulatory act' — the applicants still needed to show individual concern. This significantly limited the Article 263(4) third-limb reform.

questions.question: An applicant who cannot establish standing under Article 263 TFEU may still challenge the validity of a general EU act through which mechanism?

options.A: By filing a complaint with the European Ombudsman
options.B: By raising the illegality of the EU act before a national court (in proceedings challenging a national implementing measure), which may then make a preliminary reference to the CJEU under Article 267 TFEU
options.C: By filing a petition with the European Parliament under Article 227 TFEU
options.D: By requesting the Commission to bring an infringement action against the institution that adopted the act

questions.explanation: Where a private applicant lacks standing to bring a direct annulment action, the alternative is the indirect route: challenge the national implementing measure in national courts, plead the invalidity of the EU act on which it is based, and invite the national court to make a preliminary reference to the CJEU under Article 267 TFEU (which allows the CJEU to rule on the validity of EU acts). This was the route the CJEU endorsed in UPA as an adequate remedy — though it requires challenging a national measure, which may not always be possible or convenient.

questions.question: In Les Verts v European Parliament [1988], the Court of Justice allowed a political party to challenge European Parliament funding acts. The case is significant for what constitutional principle?

options.A: It first established that private parties have standing under Article 263
options.B: It confirmed that the EU is a 'community based on the rule of law' — all institutional acts are subject to judicial review, and the acts of the Parliament itself are reviewable (before the Lisbon Treaty expressly provided this)
options.C: It first held that political parties could be members of the EU legal order
options.D: It established the principle that EU institutions must act in good faith toward political minorities

questions.explanation: Les Verts v European Parliament (Case 294/83) is constitutionally important for two reasons: (1) the CJEU held that acts of the European Parliament itself are reviewable — even though the original EEC Treaty did not expressly include Parliament among the reviewable institutions; (2) more broadly, the Court declared that the EEC Treaty established a 'Community based on the rule of law' in which no act escapes judicial review. This is the foundational constitutional principle that all EU institutional action is subject to judicial scrutiny.

questions.question: Can a member state challenge a Directive or Regulation adopted under the ordinary legislative procedure in which it participated and voted in favour?

options.A: No — a member state that voted in favour is estopped from later challenging the act
options.B: Yes — as a privileged applicant, a member state has unconditional standing to bring annulment proceedings regardless of how it voted in the Council
options.C: Only if it did not vote and therefore was not represented at the Council meeting
options.D: Only if it raises the annulment within 30 days of the vote, not the standard two-month period

questions.explanation: Member states are privileged applicants with unconditional standing — they do not need to show any particular interest or concern. A member state that voted in favour of an act may still bring annulment proceedings against it after the vote. This might seem paradoxical but reflects that a member state's vote is a political act, while annulment proceedings are a legal remedy available as of right to privileged applicants. There is no estoppel. A change in government or legal advice may prompt a challenge even after a vote in favour.

questions.question: In Codorniu SA v Council [1994] (C-309/89), the Court of Justice allowed a private company to challenge a Regulation. How was individual concern established?

options.A: Because the company was explicitly named in the Regulation
options.B: Because the Regulation's prohibition on using 'Crémant' for Spanish sparkling wines directly and individually affected Codorníu, which had used that term as a registered trade mark since 1924 — distinguishing it from all other Spanish sparkling wine producers
options.C: Because the Regulation applied only to a small, defined group of producers
options.D: Because the company was the only Spanish sparkling wine producer affected

questions.explanation: Codorníu is a rare example of an applicant successfully meeting the Plaumann test for a Regulation. The key was that Codorníu held a registered trade mark 'Gran Cremant' dating from 1924 — a historical characteristic that distinguished it from all other producers in the class. The Regulation's effect on its trade mark rights was not generic but peculiar to Codorníu's specific legal position. This is the 'closed class' approach: when the group is defined by past conduct and closed (cannot be joined by new members), individual concern is more readily established.

questions.question: What is an 'anti-dumping case' exception to the Plaumann test, and how does it work?

options.A: Anti-dumping Regulations are exempt from judicial review because they are quasi-criminal measures
options.B: Producers and importers who participated in the Commission's anti-dumping investigation are individually concerned by the resulting Regulation because the Regulation is based on their particular situation — it is in substance a bundle of individual decisions
options.C: Anti-dumping cases use a different standing test requiring proof of financial loss
options.D: Only the exporting state, not individual producers, can challenge anti-dumping Regulations

questions.explanation: Anti-dumping Regulations are a recognised exception to the strict Plaumann approach: producers or exporters named in the Regulation or whose data was used in the investigation are individually concerned because the Regulation, though legislative in form, is based on their particular circumstances (Allied Corporation; Extramet). The Regulation is 'in reality' a bundle of decisions affecting individual companies. This 'closed class' approach permits challenge by those directly involved in the investigation even though the act is a general Regulation.

questions.question: Why does the Article 263(4) standing requirement for private parties raise concerns about compatibility with Article 47 of the Charter of Fundamental Rights (right to effective judicial protection)?

options.A: Because Article 263(4) requires excessive court fees that deny poor applicants access to justice
options.B: Because the Plaumann test effectively denies private parties access to the CJEU to challenge general EU legislative acts — the indirect route via preliminary reference does not always provide an equivalent remedy, raising doubts about whether EU law provides 'effective judicial protection'
options.C: Because the two-month time limit is too short for adequate legal preparation
options.D: Because privileged applicants have an unfair advantage over private parties

questions.explanation: The tension between the Plaumann test and Article 47 Charter is a live constitutional issue. The Plaumann test makes it very difficult for private parties to challenge EU legislative acts directly. The alternative (national court / preliminary reference) is indirect and depends on the availability of a challengeable national implementing measure. This may leave private parties without an effective remedy for EU legislative wrongs that affect them — potentially violating the right to effective judicial protection guaranteed by Article 47 Charter and Article 6(1) ECHR. The EU courts have resisted fully resolving this tension.
